question: I roll a 7 and I have 9 resource cards. I also want to play a knight card this turn. What should I do first?
expected_answer: You must handle the 7 roll first: discard 4 cards (half of 9, rounded down), then move the robber and steal. You may play the knight card at any time during your turn (including before rolling), but since you've already rolled a 7, the roll's mandatory effects must complete before you continue. After resolving the 7, you may play the knight card during your turn's trade or build phases — but note you can only play one development card per turn total, and the knight card would also move the robber again.
model_answer:
According to [Almanac: ROLLING A "7" AND ACTIVATING THE ROBBER], when you roll a "7," you must first count your resource cards. Since you have 9 resource cards, you must discard half of them, which means you will discard 4 cards. After discarding, you then move the robber and steal a resource card from another player. 

You cannot play a knight card before resolving the effects of rolling a "7." Therefore, you should first discard the cards and move the robber before playing the knight card.

question: I just built a settlement on a harbor this turn. Can I immediately use that harbor's favorable trade rate?
expected_answer: In standard play (separate trade and build phases), no — you build during the build phase after trading is done, so you cannot trade again. However, using the Combined Trade/Build Phase variant (recommended for experienced players), you can trade and build in any order during your turn, so yes — the rules explicitly state you can use a harbor on the same turn you build a settlement there.
retrieved_chunks: Almanac: INTERSECTIONS | Almanac: SET-UP, VARIABLE | Almanac: ROBBER

question: My road is 6 segments long and holds the Longest Road card. An opponent builds a settlement in the middle of it, breaking it into two branches of 4 and 2. Do I lose the Longest Road card?
expected_answer: Your road is broken. You now count only your longest continuous branch, which is 4 segments. Since 4 is less than 5, you no longer have a qualifying road at all. If another player already has a longer continuous road, they take the card. If two or more players now tie for the longest road, the card is set aside until one player has a clearly longer road of at least 5 segments.
model_answer: According to the rules, if an opponent builds a settlement that breaks your road into two separate branches, you lose the Longest Road card. The Longest Road card is awarded to the player with the longest continuous path of roads, and since your road is no longer continuous, you would no longer qualify for it. The rules don't specify this.
